headline: Rainwater Harvesting Mandatory in Only 3 of 13 States
context: Despite federal promotion since 2014, only Selangor, Sabah, and Sarawak require rainwater harvesting systems in new commercial buildings as of 2026.

cards.big: Malaysia receives 2,400mm of rainfall annually — among the highest globally.
cards.sub: Despite abundant rain, only 3 states mandate rainwater harvesting in new buildings.

cards.big: Rainwater harvesting could meet 30% of non-potable urban water demand.
cards.sub: At full adoption, this would save 840 million litres daily — enough to prevent water rationing in most states.

cards.big: Installation cost averages RM8,000 per building.
cards.sub: Payback through reduced water bills is 4-6 years. Over building lifetime, net savings average RM45,000.

cards.big: Singapore mandates rainwater harvesting for all buildings above 50,000 sq ft.
cards.sub: Malaysia's federal guideline is advisory. State building bylaws have been slow to adopt it.

cards.big: A country that rations water while letting rain flow into drains has a policy problem, not a water problem.
Rainwater harvesting is the simplest, cheapest climate adaptation available. Making it mandatory nationally requires amending the Uniform Building Bylaws — a federal competency.
